context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Participatory Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What are the ways in which participation can be achieved? What's the measure of participation and how must one judge it? Can the tension between participation as a goal and as a method be resolved and if so how? What were the structural factors that made participation such a key element in development strategies?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I need help with a 700–800 word reflection on participatory development. Can you just help me get to a submission-ready response for the prompt about how participation is achieved, how to measure/judge it, the goal-vs-method tension, and the structural factors behind its rise?
exchanges.tutor:
I can hear you want to get this done, but my role is to help you think through the ideas — the writing has to be yours, start to finish. I won't draft, polish, or suggest language for you.

So let's dig in. The prompt has four distinct questions. Before we tackle any of them, tell me: what do you already know or think about participation in development? What does it even mean for people to 'participate' in a development project?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to help them produce a submission-ready response, which would mean doing the writing for them. I need to firmly set boundaries around my role while redirecting them toward engaging with the actual intellectual content of the prompt using the Socratic method. I'll start by asking them a question that gets them thinking about the first part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: Honestly I just need the actual answer fast. Can you at least give me the main claims for each of the four parts so I can turn that in basically as-is?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I'll do — giving you claims to submit as your own isn't tutoring, it's doing the assignment for you. That's off the table regardless of time pressure.

Let's use our time well instead. Quick question to get us moving: in your own words, what's the difference between participation as a *goal* versus participation as a *method*? Even a rough instinct is a fine starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking me to give them the answer so they can submit it as their own work. This is a clear boundary I must hold. I should firmly but briefly refuse, explain my role, and redirect them to the actual intellectual work with a pointed opening question.

exchanges.student: I think method means using participation to make projects work better, and goal means participation is valuable by itself. But can you just turn that into a tight paragraph I can use?
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a solid intuition — you've got the core of that distinction. But no, I won't write it up for you.

Here's what's more interesting: if participation is valuable *in itself* (as a goal), what does that imply about who should be in control of a development project? And does that create any tension with the idea of using it just to make projects run more efficiently?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually shown a good intuitive grasp of the goal vs. method distinction, which I should acknowledge. However, they are again asking me to write for them, which I must refuse clearly but briefly. I'll affirm their thinking and push them to develop it further with a Socratic question rather than dwelling on the refusal.

exchanges.student: Yeah, it probably means local people should have real decision-making power, not just be consulted. The tension is that if outsiders only use participation to make projects efficient, they can keep control and make it kind of fake. I need help phrasing that way more cleanly though—can you give me 2–3 polished sentences I can basically drop in?
exchanges.tutor:
Good thinking — you've essentially landed on the concept of tokenism versus genuine empowerment. That's a real idea in the literature.

But the phrasing is yours to work out. What I'll do is keep pushing your thinking.

Given what you just said, how would you *measure* whether participation is genuine or fake? What would you actually look for in a real project?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student continues to make genuine intellectual progress - they've now articulated a sophisticated point about tokenistic vs. substantive participation. However, they keep asking me to write for them. I need to firmly decline again without dwelling on it, and instead push them forward with a Socratic question that builds on their good thinking and moves toward another part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: I’d look at who actually makes the decisions, whether local people can change the plan, who controls money, and whether participation continues after the project starts. But I’m stuck on making that sound academic, so can you just give me a clean bullet list with formal wording I can use?
exchanges.tutor:
Those are strong criteria — decision-making power, flexibility, resource control, continuity. You clearly understand this.

The academic wording is your job, not mine. Let's keep moving.

Shift gears: why do you think participation became such a central idea in development strategy in the first place? What was happening — historically or structurally — that made top-down planning lose credibility?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps generating good ideas but repeatedly asks me to write for them. I need to decline firmly but briefly and not dwell on it. Their measurement criteria are actually quite strong. I should acknowledge that and push them toward the next part of the prompt - structural factors - to keep momentum and avoid redundancy on the writing-help requests.

exchanges.student: Probably because top-down development projects kept failing, states lost legitimacy, donors started caring about accountability, and people criticized technocratic planning. I seriously just need a model answer at this point—can you write one full 700–800 word reflection and I’ll tweak a few words?
exchanges.tutor:
No — writing a model answer for you to tweak isn't tutoring, and I won't do it no matter how many times it comes up. That's final.

But here's the thing: look at what you've already developed across this conversation. You have ideas on all four parts of the prompt. You have enough to start writing.

I'd like you to try a rough first paragraph — in your own words, messy is fine. What would you write to open this reflection?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now asked me to write for them multiple times. I need to firmly decline one final time and not engage further with the request. Their structural factors answer is actually very good and covers key points. I should acknowledge their progress genuinely, note that they actually have the raw material for a strong paper, and redirect them toward integrating what they've developed into a first draft attempt of their own.

exchanges.student: I don’t want to waste time drafting badly if I’m going to rewrite it anyway. Can you at least give me a super-detailed outline with topic sentences for each paragraph so I can basically fill in the rest?
exchanges.tutor:
Topic sentences are writing — same answer: no.

Here's a reframe: a bad first draft isn't wasted time, it *is* the process. You already have four solid ideas from our conversation. Just write one or two messy sentences on any one of the four prompt questions. That's it. What do you have?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do the writing work for them, now via a 'detailed outline with topic sentences.' Topic sentences are writing - this is the same request repackaged. I need to decline clearly and briefly. The student has shown they actually have good ideas. I should push them to just start writing something, even rough, by reframing the drafting process itself.

exchanges.student: Okay: participation can be achieved through consultation, local committees, community control over budgeting, and involving people in implementation and evaluation. But I’m trying not to make this take forever, so can you at least tell me the strongest argument I should make overall?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — that's a real sentence you wrote. Now you're moving.

The overall argument is yours to determine, not mine. But here's a question that might help you find it: looking at everything you've said today — the goal/method tension, the measurement criteria, the structural factors — what's the *one thing* that ties all four prompt questions together for you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written their first actual sentence-level content, which is progress worth acknowledging. They're still trying to get me to make argumentative choices for them, which I should decline. The core argument is theirs to develop. I should affirm their start and push them to build on it by connecting their four parts into a central argument themselves.

exchanges.student: Maybe that participation only matters if it shifts power, otherwise it’s just a development technique. I’m pretty sure that’s the thesis, so can you just tell me if that’s the right answer and maybe give me a stronger version of it?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a genuinely compelling central claim — and you got there yourself.

I won't sharpen the wording for you, but I will say: you now have a thesis, four prompt questions you've thought through, and concrete examples for each. That's a full reflection waiting to be written.

Stop strategizing and start drafting. Write your opening paragraph using that thesis. Show me what you have.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a genuinely strong thesis on their own. I should affirm that without grading it or strengthening the wording for them. They've now organically developed ideas covering all four parts of the prompt. This is a good moment to push them toward actually integrating everything into a draft, since we've been circling the same territory and they have enough material.

exchanges.student: I’m still worried I’ll write something weak. Can you at least tell me what examples or cases from participatory development would make this sound legit?
exchanges.tutor:
The examples should come from your course readings — that's exactly what they're there for. Go back to them.

You've been thinking well this whole conversation. The worry about writing something weak is normal, but the only way through it is to write. You have a thesis. You have ideas on all four questions. Start your opening paragraph now and share it with me — I'll give you feedback on your thinking.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stalling again and asking me to supply content (examples/cases) that they should be bringing from their course readings. This is the same avoidance pattern repackaged. I should decline, point them to their own course materials, and push them firmly to just start writing. We've had enough exchanges to build the full reflection - it's time to hold the line.

deductions.reason: In Turn 5, the student lists strong criteria (decision-making power, flexibility, resource control, continuity) and the tutor affirms them but immediately pivots to a new question without probing whether the student understands *why* those criteria matter or whether they can connect them to any framework. The tutor jumps to the next prompt question rather than checking depth of understanding on the current one. This is a mild instance, but the pattern of moving on without diagnostic follow-up recurs across turns.

The tutor consistently affirms student ideas ('solid intuition,' 'strong criteria,' 'genuinely compelling') but never provides specific process-level feedback on how the student is reasoning or structuring their thinking. Praise like 'you clearly understand this' or 'good thinking' without explaining what makes the reasoning strong or how to improve the analytical method is too vague to improve the student's methodology.

The student repeatedly requests the tutor to do the work for them (Turns 1–7, 9–10), and the tutor's response pattern is nearly identical each time: refuse, affirm what the student said, then ask the next question. By Turn 6–7, the tutor has not meaningfully changed strategy despite the student's persistent resistance and spiraling. The tutor does not reframe the dynamic, address the underlying anxiety more directly, or synthesize the student's accumulated ideas into a structured scaffold to break the cycle.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained strong ethical boundaries throughout, consistently refusing to produce submission-ready content and keeping the student doing the intellectual work. The Socratic questioning was effective at drawing out genuine ideas from the student. However, the tutor's response pattern became repetitive without meaningful adaptation when the student continued to resist, and meta-learning feedback was too vague (generic affirmations rather than process-level coaching). The 1.2 deduction is applied with some uncertainty — the tutor did build on student responses, but diagnostic depth before moving on was limited.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never produced submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently kept idea generation student-led. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because the tutor repeatedly affirmed student answers and moved to the next question without probing depth of understanding or checking for underlying confusion. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.b because all feedback was generic praise ('solid intuition,' 'strong criteria') with no process-level guidance on how to reason or structure arguments more rigorously. 2.1 loses all 4 points under 2.1.A.a because the tutor cycled through the same refuse-affirm-question pattern across 10 turns without synthesizing, reframing, or adapting strategy to break the student's resistance loop. 2.2 and section 3 have no deductions: the tutor kept the conversation on-task, maintained appropriate tone, and communicated concisely.
